old_claim.note: Earth System Models provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct edge remains from satellite weather forecasting to Earth system models because the 1960 TIROS forecasting scope predates coupled carbon-cycle Earth system models.

source_shape.source_locator: NOAA NESDIS anniversary history: TIROS-1 gave forecasters the first view of cloud formations developing around the globe.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source supports the satellite-weather-forecasting node through weather-satellite observation in 1960, not through later coupled Earth system models.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: A source-backed 1960 satellite observation workflow cannot depend directly on the Earth-system-model node after that node is scoped to later coupled carbon-cycle climate modeling.

ontology_before: The graph modeled 1960 satellite weather forecasting as downstream of Earth system models.
ontology_after: Satellite weather forecasting remains downstream of weather satellites and satellite remote sensing, while Earth system models stay as later coupled climate/ecosystem modeling infrastructure.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: satellite_weather_forecasting must not directly depend on earth_system_models under the current TIROS-era forecasting scope.
would_reject_if: A source shows the satellite_weather_forecasting node is intentionally scoped to modern Earth-system-model assimilation workflows rather than TIROS-era forecasting.
The Earth system model node is split and a pre-1960 numerical weather model prerequisite is introduced with a source-backed chronology.
A source demonstrates that coupled Earth system models were an enabling component of the first operational satellite weather forecasting workflow.
short_reason: TIROS-era satellite forecasting should not depend on later coupled Earth-system models.
